abstract:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is a ubiquitous endocrine disruptor found in everyday consumer products. In response to mounting safety concerns and strict new legislation, the manufacturing industry has shifted toward using various BPA analogues. While these substitutes possess the necessary polymer-forming properties, they remain significantly less studied than the original compound. Emerging evidence indicates that several of these analogues mimic BPA's behavior, disrupting the endocrine system by interacting with key nuclear receptors, including the estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocorticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X (PXR) receptors.

Recently, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) issued a pivotal re-evaluation of BPA, slashing the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to just 0.2 ng/kg—a two-million-fold reduction. This drastic shift was prompted by growing evidence of BPA’s toxicity, particularly its ability to compromise immune system integrity. Consequently, we conducted a comprehensive review of the immunomodulatory effects of the most prevalent BPA analogues in our environment. Our findings suggest that these substitutes can interfere with both innate and adaptive immunity. Such disruptions may contribute to a range of immune-mediated conditions, including hypersensitivity, allergies, and significant alterations to the human microbiome.
